primary_diagnosis.name: Minor superficial (partial-thickness) friction abrasion of the right knee, contaminated with embedded gravel; subcutaneous fat intact

pathophysiology_summary: A tangential high-friction shearing force (a fall onto concrete) has sheared off the epidermis and superficial dermis of the anterior knee, producing a partial-thickness abrasion ("road rash"). Exposed dermal nerve endings account for the intense stinging pain, and disrupted dermal capillaries produce serous ooze and pinpoint capillary bleeding; the subcutaneous fat remains intact, confirming the injury is partial-thickness rather than full-thickness. The wound is contaminated: grit and gravel are impacted in the fibrinous exudate. Two consequences drive management beyond simple dressing. First, particulate matter left in the dermis becomes permanently trapped as new epithelium closes over it, causing traumatic tattooing (permanent skin staining) and acting as a nidus for secondary bacterial infection — so thorough decontamination (irrigation/debridement) is time-sensitive. Second, any wound contaminated with soil/foreign material is tetanus-prone; with an immunisation status believed to be more than ten years old, a tetanus-containing booster is indicated. Uncomplicated partial-thickness abrasions otherwise re-epithelialise within 7–14 days under a moist, occlusive dressing, which speeds healing and reduces scarring compared with letting the wound dry and scab.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic mechanism (friction/shearing fall onto concrete) with immediate raw, weeping, stinging superficial skin loss.
Must be excluded — but there is no gaping wound edge and no exposed subcutaneous fat, fascia or muscle; depth is superficial.
Visible embedded gravel that the patient cannot remove — a genuine component of this presentation, not an alternative diagnosis, driving the need for in-person debridement.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the priority is recognising a minor superficial abrasion while keeping the depth question (full-thickness) open until answered.

differential.diagnosis: superficial contaminated friction abrasion, uncomplicated (no infection)
differential.evidence_basis: Redness confined to the wound margins, no spreading erythema, no purulence, no systemic signs — infection is not established.
Erythema is strictly marginal to a fresh traumatic wound without proximal spread, warmth beyond the margin, purulence or fever — excluded at presentation, but a key safety-net over the coming days.
No fluctuant swelling over the patella; the process is a superficial skin injury, not bursal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen (spread of redness, pus, streaking, fever, depth) is negative — confirming a low-acuity presentation whose only serious levers are tetanus prophylaxis and decontamination of embedded gravel.

differential.diagnosis: minor superficial friction abrasion of the knee, contaminated with embedded gravel, tetanus-prone, no infection
differential.evidence_basis: Partial-thickness, fat intact, marginal erythema only; management is decontamination + moist dressing + tetanus booster + infection safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A pass recognises a minor abrasion, gives correct first-aid self-care, arranges in-person wound cleaning/debridement and a tetanus booster, and safety-nets for infection — without over-escalating to ED/000.

red_flags.description: Embedded gravel/foreign debris the patient cannot irrigate out — risk of retained foreign body, traumatic tattooing and secondary infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Particulate matter trapped as epithelium closes over it causes permanent staining and seeds infection; thorough in-person irrigation/debridement is the key time-sensitive intervention telehealth cannot deliver.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent traumatic tattooing and/or wound infection/abscess requiring later procedural care.

red_flags.description: Tetanus-prone contaminated wound with uncertain immunisation status believed to be more than ten years old
red_flags.clinical_significance: A soil/gravel-contaminated wound is tetanus-prone; if the last tetanus-containing dose is >10 years ago (or uncertain), a booster is indicated and cannot be given over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Tetanus — a rare but potentially fatal, wholly preventable complication.

red_flags.description: Developing wound infection / cellulitis or lymphangitis (spreading erythema, warmth, purulent discharge, red streak toward the groin, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: None present at first contact; this is the principal deterioration to safety-net for over the following days.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis or lymphangitis can progress to systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Occult full-thickness injury (gaping wound, exposed fat/tendon/bone) or neurovascular compromise distal to the wound
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would change management to primary closure/surgical review; excluded here (superficial, fat intact, normal distal movement and sensation) but cannot be fully confirmed remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed deep injury could lead to poor closure, tendon involvement or delayed repair.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a partial-thickness abrasion as an emergency and directing the patient to ED or 000. This is a common, self-limiting primary-care complaint — the correct disposition is self-care plus a prompt in-person wound clean/tetanus, not emergency escalation.

Focusing on the dressing and forgetting to ask about tetanus status. A contaminated wound with uncertain/lapsed immunisation needs a booster — the one genuinely serious, preventable risk in an otherwise trivial injury.

Advising a dressing over visible embedded grit without arranging proper decontamination. Retained particulate causes traumatic tattooing and infection; if the patient cannot rinse it out, in-person irrigation/debridement is needed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Recommending the wound be left to dry/scab or dressed with dry gauze
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising to ‘air it out’ or applying dry gauze directly to raw dermis. A moist, non-adherent dressing heals partial-thickness wounds faster with less scarring; dry gauze adheres and re-injures the wound bed on removal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Routine antibiotics for an uninfected wound
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing prophylactic oral or topical antibiotics for a clean, uninfected abrasion. Not indicated; decontamination and dressing are the treatment, with antibiotics reserved for established infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True depth of the wound and definitive exclusion of full-thickness injury on direct inspection/probing
Complete removal of embedded gravel (requires high-pressure irrigation and mechanical debridement)
Palpation for bony step-off, joint effusion or deep foreign body
Administration of a tetanus-containing booster (an injection the AI Doctor cannot give)
Exact blood pressure, heart rate and temperature by clinician measurement
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a minor superficial abrasion; guide immediate first-aid self-care (gentle irrigation under clean running water, simple analgesia, a non-adherent moist dressing, avoid dry gauze); and arrange PROMPT in-person care with a GP or practice nurse (within 24–48 hours) for thorough irrigation/debridement of the embedded gravel and a tetanus-containing booster given the contaminated wound and uncertain status. Safety-net clearly for infection. Do NOT escalate to ED/000 for this injury.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (mechanism and appearance are typical). The real risks are (a) OVER-action — sending a minor scrape to ED, and (b) UNDER-action — pure at-home self-care that omits the tetanus booster and decontamination of retained gravel.
